3 min read
Just now

--

If you’ve ever downloaded a .jsx file, your first instinct was probably to double-click it. Instead of seeing a webpage, Windows either asks which app to use or opens the file as plain text.
This is one of the most common frustrations for developers, students, and anyone working with AI-generated code.
The good news is that you don’t always need to install an entire React development environment just to see what’s inside a JSX file.
What Is a JSX File?
A JSX file contains JavaScript with HTML-like syntax. It’s commonly used in React applications to describe user interfaces.
Unlike regular HTML files, JSX isn’t understood directly by web browsers. It usually needs to be compiled by tools like Babel or bundled by frameworks such as React, Vite, or Next.js before it can be rendered.
That’s why double-clicking a JSX file in Windows doesn’t display the page you’re expecting.
Why People Need to Open JSX Files
There are many situations where you simply want to inspect a JSX file instead of building a complete React project.
For example:

• You asked ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini to generate a React component.

• You downloaded a UI template from GitHub.

• A teammate sent you a JSX component for review.

• You want to verify the layout before integrating it into your application.

• You only need to inspect the generated HTML and assets.
In these cases, setting up Node.js, npm packages, and a development server can be unnecessary.
The Traditional Approach
Most tutorials recommend installing:

• Node.js

• npm

• React

• Vite or Create React App

• Visual Studio Code
While this is the correct workflow for developing React applications, it’s often excessive if your goal is simply to preview a single file.
A Faster Way to Preview JSX Files
This is where PreviewKit becomes useful.
PreviewKit is a Windows desktop application designed to preview modern frontend files without requiring a complete project setup.
With PreviewKit, you can:

• Open JSX files directly.

• Preview the rendered output.

• Inspect the generated HTML.

• Compare two JSX files side by side.

• View images, videos, stylesheets, and other resources used by the page.

• Quickly review AI-generated frontend code before using it in your project.
Instead of spending time configuring a development environment, you can immediately focus on understanding the code and its visual output.
Working with AI-Generated React Components
Large language models such as ChatGPT and Claude can generate React components within seconds. However, reviewing those components is often the next challenge.
Before copying code into your application, it’s helpful to verify:

• Does the layout render correctly?

• Are all images loading?

• Are videos and other assets referenced properly?

• Does the generated HTML match your expectations?

• How does this version compare with a previous one?
Being able to preview and compare files quickly can save a significant amount of development time.
When PreviewKit Is Most Helpful
PreviewKit is especially useful if you regularly work with:

• JSX

• TSX

• HTML

• Vue Single File Components

• Markdown

• JSON
Whether you’re a frontend developer, student, technical writer, QA engineer, or AI-assisted coder, having a dedicated preview tool can simplify your workflow.
Final Thoughts
Opening a JSX file shouldn’t require creating a new React project every time.
If your goal is to inspect, preview, or compare frontend files quickly, using a dedicated preview tool is often the simplest solution.
PreviewKit helps you open JSX files, inspect their rendered output, compare different versions, and explore the resources used by your frontend pages — all from a Windows desktop application.
As AI-generated frontend code becomes more common, having a fast way to review JSX files before integrating them into a project is becoming increasingly valuable.
